title: Hotfix: prevent burst starvation under telemetry lag
preview: Context: During Dedicated burst beta we observed brief "denial storms" where one tenant would get disproportionately denied burst admissions for ~30–90s after a telemetry delay event. This manifested as burst capacity being available (per fleet dashboards) but the admission controller continuing to deny with reason=POOL_SATURATED / FAIRNESS_THROTTLE for that tenant, while other tenants recovered faster. Root cause: the fairness + pool-availability calculation in the admission controller was using a raw (unsmoothed) free-capacity signal derived from fleet telemetry. When telemetry arrived late/out-of-order, the computed free_capacity could transiently drop to ~0 and stay pinned until the next sample; the token-bucket refill path treated that as hard saturation and stopped refilling, effectively starving tenants that had recently spent tokens. Fix: (1) add EWMA smoothing and monotonic guards for free-capacity + utilization signals, (2) add telemetry staleness detection (age > telemetry_max_age_ms) and switch to a conservative "stale mode" that prevents starvation by allowing minimal refill and limiting denial amplification, while still respecting per-tenant burst caps and global fleet safety caps, and (3) add explicit guard conditions to avoid negative/implausible deltas causing refill=0 for an entire window. This PR is intentionally scoped as a hotfix: no API shape changes, no new config required for existing tenants; defaults are safe and feature-flagged (burst.telemetry_smoothing_enabled) for rollback. Test plan: unit tests for smoothing + stale mode; simulation test that replays out-of-order telemetry and verifies no tenant sees >X consecutive denials solely due to telemetry staleness; staging canary with synthetic telemetry lag injection. Rollout: enable flag for internal dogfood tenants first, then beta allowlist (us-east/H100) after 24h with no SLO regression; keep kill-switch available via config bundle.

title: Add runbook links to experiment alerts + Console guardrail banners
preview: Problem: During beta we saw multiple slow triage loops where the on-call (and Support) had to ask around for the right runbook when an experiment guardrail alert fired (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant skew, etc.). We already have runbooks in Confluence, but the link isn’t present where the incident starts: alert payloads and the Console guardrail banners. This PR wires canonical runbook URLs into (1) alert payload annotations (so PagerDuty/Slack notifications carry a direct ‘Runbook’ link) and (2) Console guardrail banners on the experiment details/results pages. Scope: route experiments only (no global SRE alert conventions refactor in this PR). What changed: - Added a small, versioned mapping of {alert_key -> runbook_url} for experiment-related alerts (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant 0 traffic, allocation skew, p99 regression auto-stop). - Plumbed runbook_url through the alert/notification payload used by the guardrail evaluator and the notifications pipeline. - Updated Console guardrail banner model to accept an optional runbook_url and render a ‘View runbook’ CTA when present. - Added safeguards: if an alert key is unknown or the URL is missing/invalid, we omit the link (no broken UI, no malformed payload). - Added unit tests for mapping + URL validation and an integration test that asserts the runbook link is present end-to-end for a representative guardrail event. Runbook sources (canonical): - Experiment assignment debugging runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Assignment+Debugging+Runbook - Experiment health dashboards spec (links out to dashboards/runbooks): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Health+Dashboards+Spec Notes: - This intentionally does not change Grafana rule definitions in observability-pack; it only ensures Redwood-originated notifications + Console surfaces carry the runbook URL. (Observability-pack PRs can independently add runbook_url annotations at rule level.) - URL allowlist: limited to confluence.redwoodinference.com to avoid arbitrary links in alert payloads. - Console UI is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; the banner rendering is a no-op if the backend doesn’t send runbook_url. Testing: - Unit: GuardrailAlertRunbookMapTest, UrlValidationTest - Integration: ExperimentGuardrailNotificationE2E (asserts runbook_url in notification payload + Console API response) Rollout: safe to ship with no config migrations; if we need to change runbook URLs, we can update the mapping and redeploy. Follow-ups: - Add runbook_url annotation in observability-pack alert rules so Grafana-native alerts match Redwood notifications. - Expand the mapping to cover non-experiment Rollouts alerts after GA if this proves useful.

title: Integrate Smart Routing with burst admission decisions (fallback + backpressure)
preview: Motivation: Dedicated burst capacity requires routing to consult the Burst Admission Controller before borrowing from shared burst pools, and to behave safely when burst is denied (protecting non-burst tenants + preserving determinism). This PR wires the routing/gateway path into the admission controller, adds deterministic policy evaluation inputs, and defines the fallback/backpressure behaviors for denied/timeout cases. Summary of behavior: For Dedicated tenants with burst enabled, routing computes a BurstContext (tenant_id, region, hw_class, baseline allocation id, requested_concurrency/requested_tokens estimate, priority tier) and calls the Burst Admission Controller synchronously at request admission time. If admitted, routing tags the request with the admission grant_id and burst_pool_id and routes to the burst pool endpoint group; if denied, routing falls back to baseline dedicated capacity when available (no pool borrowing) and otherwise applies backpressure (HTTP 429 with structured denial reason + Retry-After) rather than retrying across neighbors. Determinism: The admission request includes a stable request_key (derived from gateway request_id + model_route + tenant_id) and a policy_snapshot_hash from the control-plane config cache. Routing does not “shop around” across pools/regions/hardware classes. For the initial release, burst is strictly within-region and within hardware class. Failure modes: If the admission controller call times out or returns 5xx, routing treats it as a deny with reason=admission_unavailable and routes only to baseline dedicated capacity; if baseline is saturated, we backpressure with 503 (no burst) to avoid accidental neighbor impact. Observability: Adds routing-side counters and latency histograms for admission calls, denials by reason, and fallback usage, and propagates denial reason codes to the client error surface (aligned with pr-23795). Notes: This PR intentionally does not implement queueing-on-deny; the chosen semantics for v1 are immediate decision + client-driven retry using Retry-After guidance. Queueing can be revisited once we have stronger end-to-end SLO evidence and per-tenant buffering policies.

title: Route experiments: guardrail evaluator (soft-stop) + notifications
preview: This adds the initial guardrail evaluation pipeline for route-level experiments. The evaluator computes guardrail status per {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} from the existing experiment rollups and updates experiment status to DEGRADED when thresholds are breached. In this PR the system is intentionally soft-stop only: we do NOT stop routing traffic or mutate allocation automatically. Instead we (1) mark the experiment as DEGRADED with a reason payload suitable for Console, (2) emit audit + internal events, and (3) notify the owning team via Slack (and optionally email via notifier integration). Motivation: beta customers and internal teams need a consistent way to catch regressions quickly (p99 latency, error rate, cost/token) without relying on bespoke dashboards. This unblocks Console guardrail banners and the first-pass on-call playbooks without taking on the operational risk of automatic hard-stop. Follow-ups: optional hard-stop + kill switch (tracked separately), alert tuning, and quality-eval guardrails once eval scores are wired. Implementation overview: - Adds GuardrailEvaluator job in experiment-service that runs every 60s per shard, evaluating active experiments only. - Fetches aggregated metrics from analytics rollup tables (5m window by default; configurable to 1m/15m). - Compares metrics to guardrails in experiment config (per-variant thresholds; defaults applied when unspecified). - Updates experiment_status table with: status=HEALTHY|DEGRADED, last_evaluated_at, breached_guardrails list, and a stable incident_key for de-duping notifications. - Emits OTel span and a metric: route_experiments.guardrail_breaches_total{guardrail_type, route_id, experiment_id}. - Sends notifications via internal notifier service (Slack channel + message templates) with rate limiting + de-dupe (no more than once per incident_key per 30m). Guardrails included in this PR: latency_p99_ms, error_rate, cost_per_1k_tokens_usd, throughput_drop_pct (relative to control when available; otherwise absolute threshold). Non-goals: - No automatic stop / rollback (soft-stop only). - No statistical significance or long-horizon analysis. - No customer-defined custom metrics. Safety/operability: - Feature-flagged: route_experiments.guardrails_evaluator_enabled (default OFF). - Writes are idempotent and bounded; evaluator skips when analytics rollups are stale beyond 10m. - Notification rate limiting is per {org_id, experiment_id, guardrail_type}. - Adds runbook links in Slack payload (will be wired into alerts in follow-up). Testing: unit tests for threshold evaluation, de-dupe, staleness handling, and control-vs-variant deltas; integration test against local analytics fixture tables; dogfooded in staging with synthetic latency injection. Screenshots: n/a (server-side only).

title: Burst: standardize denial reason codes + surface to routing/gateway
preview: Motivation: During burst beta we’ve had multiple cases where customers/Support see generic 429/overload errors without actionable context (e.g., cap reached vs fleet safety vs cooldown). This PR standardizes a small, stable set of burst denial reason codes in the admission controller response and propagates them through routing -> gateway so we can (a) render clear customer-facing error messages, (b) expose denial breakdowns in Console and dashboards, and (c) reduce Support back-and-forth by making the denial cause inspectable. Summary of changes: 1) Introduce a versioned enum `BurstDenialReason` in `common/capacity` shared types with explicit semantics and non-overlapping categories. 2) Admission controller returns `decision=DENY` with `reason_code` and optional `retry_after_ms` for retryable denials (e.g., COOLDOWN_ACTIVE / POOL_SATURATED). 3) Routing stores the reason code in request context and includes it in structured logs + trace attributes; for HTTP responses we map denial to a consistent error payload and set `x-redwood-burst-denial-reason` header for Dedicated tenants (guarded behind feature flag). 4) Gateway maps denial reason codes to a stable `error.code` string (e.g., `burst_denied_pool_saturated`) and preserves existing status codes (429 for capacity-style denials, 503 for internal admission errors/timeouts) to avoid breaking clients. 5) Add Prometheus counter `redwood_burst_denials_total{reason=...}` with strict label set; no tenant_id label to avoid cardinality blowups. 6) Tests: unit tests for mapping table, golden tests for error payloads, and an integration test that asserts determinism: same inputs -> same denial code. Backwards compatibility: Existing clients that only look at status code/message are unaffected. Header + structured field are additive and behind `burst.denial_reasons.enabled` flag default OFF until rollout. Reason code set (initial): `TENANT_BURST_DISABLED`, `TENANT_CAP_REACHED`, `MAX_BURST_RATIO_EXCEEDED`, `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`, `POOL_SATURATED`, `FLEET_SAFETY_CAP`, `TELEMETRY_STALE`, `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR`, `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT`. Follow-ups: Console surface (ENG-4855) will use the new read API + denial breakdown; Support playbook update in ENG-4881 to align messaging and recommended retry/backoff per reason.

title: Fix missing experiment attribution on streaming retries
preview: Context: During the route experiments beta we saw a subset of streaming requests (SSE) lose experiment attribution after a retry, which caused (a) undercounted per-variant traffic, (b) misleading guardrail charts, and (c) confusing traces where the initial attempt was tagged but the retry attempt was not. This was escalated via SUP-24877 and corroborated by staging attribution-drop dashboards. Root cause: Our streaming retry path reconstructs a new RequestContext / SpanContext for the retry attempt. The base routing decision (including experiment assignment) was persisted on the first attempt, but the retry code path re-evaluated portions of the request pipeline and did not copy experiment dimensions into the retry context. In some cases we also emitted the streaming response headers from the retry attempt before experiment tags were re-attached, so x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-experiment-variant were missing even though the route was in an active experiment. Changes: (1) Persist experiment assignment (experiment_id, variant_id, config_version) into the per-request RetryState and rehydrate it into the retry RequestContext before any OTel spans/metrics are emitted. (2) Ensure the streaming response header writer reads attribution from RequestContext (not transient span tags) so retries remain consistent. (3) Add a guard to prevent re-bucketing on retry unless the original assignment is absent (maintains determinism and prevents variant flipping mid-stream). (4) Add coverage: integration test exercising streaming + injected mid-stream network failure + retry, asserting that experiment dimensions remain stable across attempts and are present on emitted spans/metrics and response debug headers. Safety/rollout: This is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; behavior only changes when retries occur. Added a metric counter for attribution_rehydrated_on_retry to validate impact in canary. Linked work: ADR-048 (attribution schema), PR-28470 (request context plumbing).

title: Dedicated rollouts: implement promote/abort actions + audit events (idempotent, concurrency-safe)
preview:
Context: Dedicated staged rollouts need deterministic, auditable stage-by-stage promotion and a safe abort path. This PR implements the action endpoints on the dedicated-pool-rollouts service, wires audit event emission, and adds correctness guardrails (idempotency keys + optimistic concurrency) to make promotion safe under retries and operator concurrency.

What’s included: (1) New actions: POST /v1/dedicated_pools/{pool_id}/rollout_plans/{plan_id}:promote and POST /v1/dedicated_pools/{pool_id}/rollout_plans/{plan_id}:abort. (2) Deterministic state transitions + validation: only RUNNING|HELD plans can be promoted; only RUNNING|HELD can be aborted; COMPLETED/ABORTED are terminal. Promotion increments stage_index (monotonic), records promoted_at, and snapshots gate evaluation summary at time of action. Abort transitions to ABORTED and reverts active stage routing intent to the last known-good baseline (control plane intent only; data plane applies via existing reconciler). (3) Audit events: emits dedicated_rollout.promote and dedicated_rollout.abort via the central audit pipeline; payload includes actor, pool_id, rollout_plan_id, previous_stage, new_stage, reason (required), request_id, idempotency_key, and concurrency token. (4) Idempotency: supports Idempotency-Key header (and x-redwood-idempotency-key alias for legacy clients) for promote/abort; stores action result keyed by (rollout_plan_id, action_type, idempotency_key) with a 24h TTL; repeated requests return the original response with a 200 and an Idempotency-Replayed: true header. (5) Optimistic concurrency: supports If-Match: W/"rollout-plan-rev:{n}" (preferred) and request body field expected_revision (compat); mismatches return 409 Conflict with the current revision and a human-readable message to prevent double-promote under retries or two operators clicking promote.

API/behavior details: Promote requires reason in the request body; it returns the updated rollout plan resource (including stage_index and revision) plus action metadata (action_id, replayed flag). Abort requires reason and optional abort_mode (best_effort|strict); strict will fail closed if the plan is not reconcilable (e.g., missing baseline config), best_effort will still mark ABORTED and rely on operator follow-up; default is strict for now (per SRE/security review).

Testing: Added unit tests for state transitions, idempotency replay, and revision conflicts; added an integration-style test using the in-memory DB harness to cover promote->promote (replay) and promote->abort interactions. Manual testing in dev: exercised endpoints via adminctl against a test Dedicated pool, verified audit events show up in audit-viewer with required fields.

Notes: This PR intentionally does not implement force-promote/hold; those are tracked separately and require explicit admin-only RBAC and additional audit constraints. Gate evaluation is read-only here; this PR only snapshots the gate summary into the audit event and rollout action record.

Abort requires reason and optional abort_mode (best_effort|strict); strict will fail closed if the plan is not reconcilable (e.g., missing baseline config), best_effort will still mark ABORTED and rely on operator follow-up; default is strict for now (per SRE/security review).

Testing: Added unit tests for state transitions, idempotency replay, and revision conflicts; added an integration-style test using the in-memory DB harness to cover promote->promote (replay) and promote->abort interactions. Manual testing in dev: exercised endpoints via adminctl against a test Dedicated pool, verified audit events show up in audit-viewer with required fields.

Notes: This PR intentionally does not implement force-promote/hold; those are tracked separately and require explicit admin-only RBAC and additional audit constraints. Gate evaluation is read-only here; this PR only snapshots the gate summary into the audit event and rollout action record.

title: [LTS 1.18] Fix Sev1 crash in KV cache compaction (backport)
preview:
Context
This is a targeted backport to runtime-1.18-lts for a Sev1 crash in KV cache compaction reported during LTS pilot validation and via SUP-18544. The crash manifests as a use-after-free / invalid access during compaction when a block is concurrently scheduled for eviction under memory pressure. We are treating this as LTS-eligible (correctness + crash) and release-blocking for 1.18.1-lts RC.

Root cause
Compaction iterates over a segment list while eviction can detach/free segments after a memory-pressure callback. On main, this was mitigated by refcounted pinning and a stricter lock ordering (compaction acquires segment_lock before eviction_lock). In the 1.18 LTS branch, we still had the older code path where compaction relied on "best-effort" pointer stability and a non-atomic "in_compaction" flag, which is insufficient under bursty workloads with aggressive eviction.

Fix
- Introduce segment pinning during compaction: compaction now takes a strong ref to the segment header before walking blocks; eviction checks pin count and defers freeing until pins drop.
- Enforce lock ordering to avoid a rare deadlock we saw in a local stress test when trying the naive fix (documented in code comment).
- Harden guardrails: if compaction detects a segment state transition mid-walk (evict_pending), it bails out and retries rather than continuing with stale offsets.

Why safe for LTS
- Minimal diff relative to mainline fix; avoids bringing in unrelated KV cache changes that landed after 1.18 branch cut.
- No behavior change on the steady state path except an extra atomic inc/dec around segments being compacted.
- Added focused tests + ran perf gates to validate no measurable regression within LTS thresholds.

Testing
- Added unit test that reproduces the crash by interleaving compaction + eviction on a mocked clock and deterministic executor.
- Added GPU smoke test variant that forces compaction every N tokens with small KV budget to trigger frequent eviction.
- Ran scheduler regression suite (release branch) to ensure no cross-component fallout.

Perf validation notes (perf-canary)
Benchmarks ran on A100-80G and H100, workloads: chat-mixed (short+long), summarization-long, and embeddings-batch. Results vs runtime-1.18.0-lts baseline: 
- Throughput: within -0.3% to +0.2% (noise)
- p95 latency: +0.4% worst-case on summarization-long (within gate)
- Memory: no increase in steady-state KV footprint; transient pinning increases peak pinned segments by <= 2 in stress mode only

Rollout / release notes
- This should be called out under "Stability" in 1.18.1-lts release notes: "Fixes a rare but high-severity crash in KV cache compaction under eviction pressure."
- No config changes required.

References
- Jira: SUP-18544
- Related LTS epic: ENG-4821

- [x] Backport eligibility confirmed (Sev1 crash)
- [x] Focused regression test added
- [x] GPU smoke test executed on release branch
- [x] Perf-canary run and within LTS thresholds
- [x] SRE notified for pilot rollout monitoring
- [x] Changelog note drafted for 1.18.1-lts

title: Expedited rejoin stabilizer and controlled load-trim hotfix
preview: Motivation: Following a recent outage reconstruction (SUP-4512) we observed an oscillation during regional rejoin where multiple nodes aggressively tried to reclaim tenant KV slots, triggering cascade redistributions and transient SLO violations. This PR introduces a short-term hotfix to: 1) introduce a gentle rejoin backoff window (per-tenant jittered delay + exponential cap) to avoid synchronized reclaims; 2) add a controlled load-trim path that softly sheds low-priority traffic during rejoin windows; and 3) emit new telemetry to make oscillation and rejoin latency visible in the Console. This change is intentionally conservative: defaults apply only during detected rejoin epochs and are disabled outside of a rejoin recovery window. Changes: - runtime/scheduler/rejoin_coordinator.go: add rejoin state machine with randomized tenant backoff and exponential cooldown logic; add tests for staggered rejoin timings. - runtime/load_trim/trimmer.go: implement priority-aware soft-trimmer that signals request proxies to shed non-critical classes via header flag and emits approximate token savings. - controlplane/telemetry/v2: add rejoin_epoch metric, tenant_rejoin_backoff_seconds histogram, and soft_trim_shed_count counter. - config/defaults.yaml: new toggle rejoin_relay.enabled (default true for this rollout window) and rejoin_relay.default_backoff_ms. - docs/ops/rejoin-playbook.md: updated playbook with diagnostic queries and rollback steps. Rollout plan: - Stage: feature gated behind rejoin_relay.enabled. - Canary: enable for 5% of tenants in us-west-2 for 2 hours, observe rejoin_epoch and token/shed metrics. - Ramp: 25% after 6 hours if no increase in 95th latency or error rate; full rollout after 24h stability. Backout: flip rejoin_relay.enabled=false; will disable new path immediately. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for coordinator state transitions - [x] e2e simulation harness run for tenant rejoin storm - [x] metrics wired to Console dashboards - [x] docs updated - [x] post-merge telemetry alert to watch for increased shed volume Monitoring and alerts: new SLO probe rule added to detect >3x baseline soft_trim_shed_count in 10m window; alert routed to SRE runbook. Notes: This is a hotfix to reduce immediate customer impact while we finalize a larger, model-aware eviction redesign. See ENG-9821 for the long-running redesign epic.

title: Backfill tooling for 2025Q1 unit economics aggregates (idempotent + throttled)
preview: Context: Finance needs QTD and quarter-close reporting in the internal Unit Economics dashboard, but the hourly aggregation job only covered data from the initial cutover onward. This PR adds an idempotent backfill tool to populate historical aggregates for 2025Q1 (and generally any arbitrary time window) while protecting the warehouse and avoiding double-counting. Scope: (1) Adds a backfill CLI (unit-econ-backfill) that replays the same aggregation logic as the hourly job over a time range; (2) Implements idempotency via per-hour partition overwrite semantics and a backfill_runs audit table; (3) Adds throttling (max concurrent partitions, adaptive sleep on warehouse pressure) and a maintenance-window mode; (4) Adds verification queries and a reconciliation helper view to compare pre/post backfill totals; (5) Updates runbook docs referenced by alerts. Idempotency model: the backfill operates on canonical hourly partitions (dt_hour UTC) in the materialized aggregate tables. For each dt_hour, it writes to a staging table and then atomically swaps/overwrites the target partition. A backfill_runs table records (run_id, requested_range, effective_partitions, rate_card_version, git_sha, started_at, completed_at, status) for auditability and for detecting partial runs. Concurrency + safety: default max_concurrency=4, with a --warehouse-safe flag that drops to 2 and increases sleep on query timeouts; --dry-run prints partition plan and estimated work; --resume uses backfill_runs to skip completed partitions; --force allows reprocessing (for rate card updates or logic changes) but still overwrites per-hour partitions to avoid duplication. Notes: We explicitly do not backfill raw metering events; we backfill derived aggregates only. This is intended for internal reporting and reconciliation and is gated behind internal authz. Testing: Added unit tests for partition planning/idempotency and integration tests against a local warehouse test container with seeded metering + rate card fixtures. Rollout: Run once for 2025-01-01..2025-03-31 (inclusive) in the analytics maintenance window; SRE will monitor warehouse saturation dashboards; Finance will validate QTD totals using the reconciliation view (linked below). Follow-ups: If Finance requests prior quarters, we can reuse the same tool; we may want a weekly scheduled backfill for late-arriving metering data, but that is out of scope for v1.

title: responses-adapter: propagate determinism/seed headers and structured-output annotations
preview: Motivation: The OpenAI Responses variants that customers adopt increasingly rely on explicit determinism/seed directives and lightweight structured-output annotations passed through the HTTP layer. Without preserving those hints across our OpenAI-compat adapter we observed divergences between direct upstream behavior and Redwood-hosted responses in customer compatibility testing (see ENG-4821). This PR implements two coordinated changes in the adapter and runtime surface to improve compatibility and reduce customer surprises: 1) deterministic/seed propagation: accept and translate OpenAI-style determinism flags (seed, deterministic, x-seed) present as either JSON fields in the Responses body or as HTTP headers, and persist them through the routing, reassembly, and streaming layers so that streaming reassembly uses the same determinism hint used by the model selection logic; 2) structured-output annotations: accept structured output schema hints (openai_schema, schema_hint, or x-output-schema) and surface them to the structured-output postprocessor and the tool-call router. Implementation summary: - HTTP adapter: new header handling for X-OpenAI-Seed, X-OpenAI-Deterministic and X-OpenAI-Output-Schema; these are normalized to internal headers X-Redwood-Seed and X-Redwood-Output-Schema during internal RPCs so that downstream components see a consistent key. - Payload parsing: Responses-compatible body parsing now reads top-level "determinism" and "seed" keys in addition to existing generation params and merges them with header values (headers take precedence). - Streaming reassembly: streaming reassembly now attaches a deterministic-context token to each stream chunk and includes seed metadata in the final reassembly envelope so consumers and logs can assert determinism and replay. This prevents a class of flaky stream reorder bugs seen during canary. - Tool/function calling: the toolcall envelope now carries the propagated schema hint on the function metadata so third-party tool runners can perform schema-aware validation/dispatch. - Backwards compatibility: adapter will ignore unknown header keys and continues to accept legacy openai-style body shapes; a compatibility shim maps older "seed_hint" fields to the new internal header where detected. - Tests and validations: unit tests for adapter parsing, integration tests exercising streaming reassembly with seeded responses, and an end-to-end canary against a mocked OpenAI-responses backend were added. Checklist: - [x] Add header normalization in adapter layer - [x] Merge seed into routing decisions (with tests) - [x] Attach seed/annotations to streaming reassembly envelope - [x] Surface schema hint to toolcall metadata - [x] Add integration tests and canary job - [x] Update docs/compat notes (short note in docs site) Related: ENG-4821 (compat issues during customer test runs), ENG-4835 (structured output rollout plan). Breaking change: none; feature is opt-in via header/body keys. Rollout plan: feature gated behind a config flag responses.adapter.enable_seed_passthrough (default off). We enabled the flag in the dedicated canary namespaces and verified downstream regression metrics before merging.

title: Enforce credential-usage-policy: KMS claim translator & compact audit zipper
preview:
Motivation: tighten operator credential usage and KMS selection so that operator/agent tokens cannot be used to access keys outside approved jurisdictions, while keeping audit trails compact and verifiable. This PR adds a lightweight RBAC v2 credential-usage policy evaluator, a KMS claim translator that maps operator claims to region-bound key selectors, and a compact audit zipper that collapses repetitive audit entries while retaining a cryptographic verification header for later replay. These features together address customer requests for stricter jurisdictional controls (Linear ENG-2148) and an incident postmortem recommendation to reduce audit storage costs without losing forensic detail (SEC-1203).

What this changes: single continuous description below —
- RBAC: add new policy predicate credential_usage.allowed_actions and integrate into decision path for operator-scoped tokens. The evaluator runs as an early deny guard in request admission (priority before quotas/ratelimit). Default deny-if-absent semantics for cross-region operations.
- KMS claim translator: a new translation layer that consumes token operator_claims.region_hints and service tags to select KMS key aliases with region affinity. It emits a signed kms_selection_proof header that downstream keystore proxies verify; translation includes a fallback chain (primary region -> tenant region -> global soft-fail) configurable by policy.
- Region pinning: request routing honors the translator's region_hint; requests with explicit residency flags are pinned to matching capacity pools and a non-matching route results in a structured audit event + rate-limited denial to avoid silent fallback.
- Compact audit zipper: stream-side component that collapses repeated audit lines (same actor/action/resource within configurable window) into a single compressed envelope while emitting an audit_index entry. The envelope includes a Merkle-root-like digest and a timestamped nonce; raw events are stored for 72 hours then replaced by the envelope for long-term retention to satisfy e-discovery while saving S3/DB costs.
- Telemetry & observability: add metrics for denied_credential_usage, kms_translation_latency, audit_zipper_compression_ratio, and a per-tenant residency_pin_rate. Traces include the translator decision span and the audit zipper span for post-incident timelines.

Checklist (implemented):
- [x] RBAC predicate + admission hook
- [x] KMS claim translator and signed selection header
- [x] Routing pin enforcement and structured deny event
- [x] Audit zipper compression logic and retention lifecycle
- [x] Unit + integration tests for policy and translator
- [x] Migration guide and feature flag (off by default)

Compatibility/rollback: Feature gated under feature.flags.credential_usage_enforcer. Rolling back flips the gate and reverts request admission to previous path; translator has a "passthrough" mode to preserve behavior during canary. Migration notes are in docs/security/credential-usage.md.

Related: linear ENG-2148, SEC-1203, and internal runbook RBAC-R1. No customer-facing API changes; header kms_selection_proof is additive and will be ignored by older proxies.

title: openai-bridge: forward client RNG-hints and support structured-schema mapping for new Responses variants
preview: Summary: Add support in the OpenAI compatibility bridge for forwarding client-specified randomness hints and an explicit rng_seed field, and introduce structured-schema mapping to better handle the newer Responses API variants. This change ensures client requests that include 'randomness_hint' or 'rng_seed' are preserved through the bridge and honored by the serving runtime when possible, and maps new structured output descriptors from OpenAI-style Responses to Redwood's structured-output parser.

title: Thrift Sherpa: guided batching and quantization calibrator
preview: Context: customers require lower per-token cost without sacrificing tail latency or regressions in output quality. Existing autotuners either over-aggressively quantize or recommend batch sizes that increase p95 for small requests. This PR introduces 'Thrift Sherpa', a guided calibrator that combines sampled production traces, a lightweight simulator of RTT and KV-cache hit rates, and a conservative cost model to propose batching shapes and quant profiles per model + route. Design summary: 1) Offline trace ingestion: a small trace extractor that samples token lengths, sequence distributions, and route metadata. 2) Workload simulator: fast heuristics to estimate batching throughput, p50/p95, and token-level cost under candidate batch shapes and quant profiles. 3) Scoring and constraints: multi-objective score (cost reduction weighted vs. latency SLO and quality delta budget) with hard constraints for routes tagged latency-sensitive. 4) Output: compact JSON recommendation bundle per model with ranked presets and a 'cautious' fallback. 5) Rollout helper: a manifest and a canary runner that performs step-rollouts with automatic rollback on regressions. This change touches the calibrator and the recommendations API; it does not change runtime serving kernels. Motivation: provide a middle ground between one-size-fits-all defaults and full manual profiling; enable product teams to safely adopt quantization and batching with measurable unit economics. Validation: synthetic replay of five representative customers plus two internal workloads; automated regression tests for the scoring function; A/B canary run in perf-canary for 72h with telemetry hooks. Checklist: - [x] trace extractor + schema - [x] simulator with KV/hit heuristics - [x] scoring engine and constraint enforcement - [x] recommendation manifest format and exporter - [x] unit tests and property tests for scoring bounds - [x] integration test with recommendations API - [x] perf-canary run and rollback criteria defined - [x] docs (internal guide + quickstart). Related: ENG-1182 for batching improvements, ENG-1247 for quant presets. Rollout: canary on non-critical tenants for 1 week, then staged release with telemetry gating to 0.5->1->2->5% traffic. Links: Linear ENG-1182 (design), perf-canary dashboard (internal link), design doc in docs/experimental/thrift-sherpa.md.

Design summary: 1) Offline trace ingestion: a small trace extractor that samples token lengths, sequence distributions, and route metadata. 2) Workload simulator: fast heuristics to estimate batching throughput, p50/p95, and token-level cost under candidate batch shapes and quant profiles. 3) Scoring and constraints: multi-objective score (cost reduction weighted vs. latency SLO and quality delta budget) with hard constraints for routes tagged latency-sensitive. 4) Output: compact JSON recommendation bundle per model with ranked presets and a 'cautious' fallback. 5) Rollout helper: a manifest and a canary runner that performs step-rollouts with automatic rollback on regressions. This change touches the calibrator and the recommendations API; it does not change runtime serving kernels. Motivation: provide a middle ground between one-size-fits-all defaults and full manual profiling; enable product teams to safely adopt quantization and batching with measurable unit economics. Validation: synthetic replay of five representative customers plus two internal workloads; automated regression tests for the scoring function; A/B canary run in perf-canary for 72h with telemetry hooks. Checklist: - [x] trace extractor + schema - [x] simulator with KV/hit heuristics - [x] scoring engine and constraint enforcement - [x] recommendation manifest format and exporter - [x] unit tests and property tests for scoring bounds - [x] integration test with recommendations API - [x] perf-canary run and rollback criteria defined - [x] docs (internal guide + quickstart). Related: ENG-1182 for batching improvements, ENG-1247 for quant presets. Rollout: canary on non-critical tenants for 1 week, then staged release with telemetry gating to 0.5->1->2->5% traffic. Links: Linear ENG-1182 (design), perf-canary dashboard (internal link), design doc in docs/experimental/thrift-sherpa.md.

title: Emit metrics + tracing for Private backup/restore operations (with error categorization)
preview: Motivation: SRE/on-call and customers need a consistent way to answer “are backups running?” and “why did the last restore drill fail?” across VPC + on-prem Private installs. We already log backup/restore steps in the installer, but logs alone are hard to alert on and don’t support reliable aggregation. This PR adds: (1) structured Prometheus metrics for backup + restore operations (success/failure, duration, bytes, last success timestamps), (2) OpenTelemetry tracing spans with consistent attributes for per-step visibility, and (3) explicit error categorization to drive dashboards/alerts and reduce pager noise. Scope: This instruments the control-plane backup/restore path (installer-driven + control-plane endpoints) and adds common helpers used by both “backup create” and “restore apply”. It does not change backup artifact format or encryption behavior. Metrics (Prometheus): - redwood_private_backup_runs_total{result,storage_backend,component,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_duration_seconds_bucket{result,storage_backend,component} (histogram) - redwood_private_backup_bytes_total{result,storage_backend,component} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{storage_backend,component,format_version} (gauge) - redwood_private_restore_runs_total{result,source_backend,target_env,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_restore_duration_seconds_bucket{result,step} (histogram) - redwood_private_restore_validation_failures_total{check_name,error_category} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_restore_errors_total{operation,step,error_category} (counter; emitted on failure paths only) Tracing (OTel): Adds a top-level span per operation: “private.backup.create” and “private.restore.apply”, with child spans for steps: preflight, quiesce, snapshot, encrypt, upload, verify, bootstrap, migrate, validate. Spans include attributes: deployment_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, storage_backend, artifact_manifest_version, backup_id/restore_id, step, result, error_category. Error categorization: Introduces a shared enum + mapping helper that classifies common failure types in a stable way for alerting/reporting. Categories include: preflight_failed, quiesce_failed, snapshot_failed, encrypt_failed, upload_failed, download_failed, verify_failed, bootstrap_failed, migrate_failed, validation_failed, compatibility_failed, permission_denied, kms_unavailable, timeout, rate_limited, unknown. Mapping is conservative: if we can’t confidently classify, we emit unknown and preserve the raw error message in logs/traces (not as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality explosions. RBAC / permissions: No new privileges are required for the control plane, but installer pods/jobs must be allowed to emit metrics to the existing collector in Private installs. This PR reuses existing OTEL exporter wiring and the in-cluster Prometheus scrape config. Testing: - Unit tests for error classification mapping and label sanitization - Integration test in staging that runs a forced failure (bad bucket creds) and asserts metrics increments + spans have error_category - Manual validation: ran backup create / restore apply in dev VPC env; verified dashboard panels populate (see follow-up in observability-pack PR). Notes on cardinality: - No metric labels include backup_id, restore_id, customer name, or raw error strings - deployment_id is included only as a tracing attribute (not a metric label) - component label is constrained to a small set (control_plane_db, control_plane_config, k8s_manifests, audit_logs_export) - step label is constrained to known step names and used only on histogram for restore duration per step (bounded) Follow-ups: - Alert rules + dashboards land in observability-pack (PR-94) - SLO definitions tracked in slo-toolkit (PR-67) - Backup status endpoint is in PR-24318 (separate) which will consume these metrics for UI surfacing

title: Private: expose backup status endpoints from control plane
preview:
Motivation: Private customers (and our own SRE/Solutions workflows) need a consistent way to surface “backup readiness” without scraping CronJob logs or inferring state from storage backends. This PR adds control-plane API endpoints that report backup status and last-success timestamps per tenant/installation, with strict authz and safe redaction (no bucket names, object keys, or secret material). This unblocks Console UI + automated restore drills to gate on “recent successful backup” and to page on backup job regressions.

What’s included: Adds a BackupStatus read model persisted by the backup orchestrator after each run (success/failure), keyed by tenant_id and environment_id; Read-only endpoints under the Private API surface; Includes timestamps (last_success_at, last_attempt_at), status enum, last_error_category + last_error_message (truncated), artifact metadata (format_version, bytes_total), and a computed “staleness” field based on configured schedule; RBAC scopes: tenant:read plus a new private:backup_status:read for installer/service automation; Optional cluster-wide endpoint for platform operators (requires internal operator role); Adds OpenAPI spec + SDK client stub used by Console; Adds audit log events for reads of backup status (low volume) and writes on backup completion.

Endpoints: GET /v1/private/tenants/{tenant_id}/backup/status -> returns latest status record for that tenant; GET /v1/private/backup/status -> operator-only list endpoint with pagination/filtering by status/staleness (intended for internal fleet dashboards; not exposed in customer docs yet).

Non-goals / out of scope: No “trigger backup” endpoint (installer/cronjob remains source of truth); No exposure of artifact locations (bucket/key) or encryption/key IDs; No attempt to infer state by reading object storage; This does not change backup/restore format.

Security + privacy notes: last_error_message is truncated to 512 chars and scrubbed via existing log redaction utility; artifact_metadata excludes backend identifiers; list endpoint is operator-only and excluded from external docs generation.

Testing: Unit tests for status model serialization, authz gates, and redaction; API integration tests covering success and failure flows; Added a contract test asserting the response schema remains backwards compatible (fields only added, never removed) to support Console; Load test sanity: list endpoint paginates and enforces max page size.

Rollout plan: Ship behind feature flag private.backupStatusEndpoints (default on for staging, off for prod) with a follow-up to enable for Private GA once Console is ready; SRE to validate via restore drill automation and dashboard wiring.

Checklist: [x] OpenAPI updated; [x] Authz reviewed (Marcus); [x] Audit logging for reads/writes; [x] Metrics emitted on write path (status_write_success, status_write_latency_ms); [x] Docs note added to internal runbook; [x] Backwards compatible response contract.

Refs: Linear ENG-9826/ENG-9827; related installer work in redwood-private-installer PR-482/PR-489; observability pack PR-94 depends on status fields last_success_at + staleness_minutes.

title: Smart Routing: add Hosted route canary split primitive (deterministic cohorting + region-aware enforcement)
preview: Context: As part of Hosted canary rollouts GA work, we need a data-plane primitive that can split traffic on a Hosted route between a baseline target and a canary target by percentage, with deterministic cohort assignment (sticky per caller) and region-aware enforcement. This PR adds a new routing primitive `canary_split` to the Smart Routing evaluation + enforcement path. The primitive is intentionally minimal: it takes two compatible upstream targets (baseline + canary), a canary weight (0-100), and a deterministic cohort key, then selects the target for a request. This will be wired into the Rollout Orchestrator/control plane in follow-ups. What’s included: (1) New route config primitive `canary_split` with validation (weights must sum to 100, baseline/canary targets must be same modality and compatible model family; canary weight can be 0-100; guardrails around missing/invalid fields). (2) Deterministic cohorting: compute `cohort_bucket` = xxhash64(<cohort_key> + <route_id> + <rollout_id?>) mod 100; bucket < canary_weight => canary else baseline. If rollout_id is omitted we still include a stable config hash so cohorting does not flip on unrelated route config changes. (3) Cohort key derivation: prefer `x-redwood-cohort-key` header (set by gateway / console preview tools); fallback to API key id; final fallback to stable client IP hash when key id is unavailable (internal-only paths). We explicitly do NOT use request body content. (4) Region-aware behavior: the split is evaluated locally in each region using the same deterministic algorithm to preserve the global percentage distribution while avoiding cross-region coordination; we include region in logs but not in the hash input to avoid region-driven cohort flipping for clients that fail over. (5) Retries/streaming handling: we ensure the chosen target is pinned for the lifetime of a request (including internal retries) by storing `selected_target` in request context; for streaming requests we do not re-evaluate mid-stream. (6) Observability hooks: adds structured routing logs fields (`route_id`, `primitive=canary_split`, `cohort=baseline|canary`, `cohort_bucket`, `canary_weight`, `target_id`) and emits a low-cardinality counter `routing.canary_split.selected` with `cohort` and `region` tags only. Metric tagging with `rollout_id` is intentionally excluded here to avoid cardinality; it will be added at the gateway/telemetry layer where we can control aggregation (tracked in ENG-9152). (7) Safety: if validation fails or canary target is unhealthy/unavailable at evaluation time, we fall back to baseline and emit `routing.canary_split.fallback_to_baseline` with a reason code. Notes / decisions: - The deterministic hash input includes route_id to avoid cross-route correlation (same cohort key should not always land in canary across all routes). - We intentionally do not include region in the hash to preserve stickiness when a client fails over between regions. - This primitive does not implement auto-stop/rollback; it only selects a target. Control plane/orchestrator will adjust `canary_weight` and state transitions. Test plan: - Unit tests for validation + cohorting distribution (bucket boundaries, 0/100%, stable behavior). - Integration tests in `smart-routing-sim` to confirm selection is pinned across retries and streaming. - Manual in staging: configure a test Hosted route with 10% canary split; send requests with fixed `x-redwood-cohort-key` and observe consistent cohort selection + logs. Rollout plan: - This ships behind `routing.enable_canary_split_primitive` flag (default off). - Console/orchestrator will not emit this primitive until backend support is enabled tenant-by-tenant. Follow-ups: - Wire `rollout_id` + cohort tags end-to-end at gateway/telemetry (ENG-9152). - Console preview + validation endpoint to render the split plan (part of orchestrator/console backend PR). - Guardrail policy enforcement (max step, soak) is out of scope here (policy engine).

title: Add trafficctl emergency GSLB weight setter for api.redwoodinference.com
preview:
### Context
During game day and recent regional brownouts, the fastest safe mitigation is to shift Hosted API traffic between regions without waiting for Terraform applies. We currently do this via manual console operations, which is error-prone.

### What this PR does
- Introduces `trafficctl`, an audited CLI wrapper around our GSLB provider API
- Adds commands to:
  - read current targets + weights
  - set weights for api.redwoodinference.com
  - set record TTL (temporary)
- Enforces RBAC via the `infra-traffic-admin` role
- Writes an audit log event for every invocation (who, what record, old/new weights)

### Usage
```
trafficctl gslb get --record api.redwoodinference.com
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 60
trafficctl gslb set-weights --record api.redwoodinference.com --weights us-east-1-gw=0,us-west-2-gw=100
trafficctl dns set-ttl --record api.redwoodinference.com --ttl 300
```

### Rollback
- Disable the `trafficctl` GitHub Actions release workflow and revert to manual console operations
- No data-plane changes are required to roll back this PR

### Changed areas
- `cmd/trafficctl/*`
- `pkg/gslb/*`
- `pkg/auditlog/*`
- `rbac/policies/infra-traffic-admin.yaml`
- `docs/runbooks/global-traffic-routing-controls.md` (synced to Confluence by docs bot)

### Review notes
- Hannah: please verify audit event fields match incident review needs
- Alex: verify gateway target naming conventions (us-east-1-gw, us-west-2-gw)

### Review conversation (excerpt)
Hannah Schmitt: Can we ensure we log *previous weights* as well as the new weights? That's critical for rollback during an incident.

Dev Patel: Yep, updated `auditlog.Event` to include `old_weights` and `new_weights` as strings (serialized).

Ethan Park: Any safety rails to prevent accidental 100% to an unknown target?

Dev Patel: Added validation: target must be in the allowlist for the record; otherwise command aborts.

Alex Martinez: Looks good. Please add a short note that TTL should be restored after the cutover.

Dev Patel: Added to docs + examples.

### Merge
- Squash merged into main
- CI: build/test/lint passed

title: openai-invoke-affinity-annotations-and-fragment-serializer
preview: Motivation: Improve OpenAI-compat tool/function-call selection by exposing an 'invoke_affinity' annotation on tool metadata and by making streaming fragments self-descriptive so tool calls are never mis-routed or partially applied by downstream coalescers. Summary: 1) Add a non-breaking 'invoke_affinity' optional annotation to the tool registry and mapper (values: high|normal|low|prefer_local) that is used by the OpenAI-compat selection heuristic to bias tool_choice without hard overrides. 2) Serialize tool invocation fragments emitted during streaming with a compact header (v1) containing id, affinity, and schema-hash so fragment coalescers and proxies can re-assemble or route fragments deterministically. 3) Update the compatibility layer to treat tool messages with affinity=high as preferred targets for low-latency lanes (dedicated pools) and affinity=prefer_local to prefer the caller's region. 4) Ensure backwards compatibility: missing invoke_affinity behaves the same as 'normal' and fragment header is optional but validated when present. Checklist: - [x] design doc + API sketch included in PR description - [x] runtime mapper + serializer implementation - [x] unit + integration tests for selection weighting and streaming reassembly - [x] docs and SDK TS surface updates - [x] migration notes and rollout plan - [x] perf microbench showing ~0.9x selector CPU and no additional p99 token latency Changes are scoped so the OpenAI-compat surface preserves the existing function calling contract while giving product teams a low-risk knob to bias routing for latency/cost. See linked Linear tickets and end-to-end test results. Migration: rollout via feature flag 'openai.invoke_affinity' for 2 weeks, collect telemetry; revert safe if any metric regressions >5% on request success or latency. Backwards compatibility: older proxies that ignore the fragment header will still receive integrity-protected JSON tool payloads; routers that see affinity will incorporate it into their weighted selection but will not change function signatures. Related: this complements the 'stream-fragment-tagging' work but focuses on tool selection semantics rather than coalescing heuristics.

title: catalog: surface model capability flags, pricing and limits for OpenAI-compat clients
preview:
Motivation: Clients using the OpenAI-compat layer and our public model listing need a single, predictable surface to discover per-model capabilities (streaming stability, function/tool-calling support, structured-output specializations) and unit economics (pricing per token, soft/hard limits and concurrency). This PR extends the model catalog API and internal catalog objects to include capability and pricing metadata, plus server-side filters that power the console and SDKs.

Summary of changes: added explicit capability flags (openai_compat, supports_streaming_stable, supports_function_calling, structured_output_profiles) to the model listing; exposed priced_units and limits metadata (cost_per_input_token, cost_per_output_token, max_tokens_per_request, recommended_concurrency, rate_limit_tier). Implemented new query params for the GET /v1/models endpoint to filter by capability, max_estimated_cost_per_k, min_stability_score, region, and quantization support. Updated the OpenAI compatibility layer to prefer models with openai_compat=true for compatibility endpoints and to fall back predictably to compatible variants when a requested capability is not available. Added docs, SDK bindings, and tests.

Why this is safe: fields are additive. No breaking schema changes for existing consumers. Default behavior for compatibility endpoints is unchanged unless clients explicitly query the new fields or filtering. We included a migration path for older model entries missing cost metadata (defaults used and surfaced as "unknown").

API additions (examples):
- GET /v1/models?capability=function_calling&region=us-east-1&max_estimated_cost_per_k=0.50 -> returns models that advertise function calling and whose cost_per_1k_tokens <= 0.50.
- New model-listing response includes keys: capabilities (list), pricing (object), limits (object), compatibility_flags (list). Example snippet in response body (condensed for PR):
  "models": [{"id": "redwood-slim-1b", "capabilities": ["embeddings","streaming"], "pricing": {"cost_per_input_token": "0.00005", "cost_per_output_token": "0.00006", "currency": "USD"}, "limits": {"max_tokens": "4096", "recommended_concurrency": "4"}, "compatibility_flags": ["openai_compat"]}]

Implementation notes:
- internal/catalog/store: extended ModelRecord with flattened string lists for capabilities and compatibility_flags; pricing/limits stored as strings to avoid schema churn in metadata blobs.
- api/handlers/models: added parsing for new query params and server-side filter combinators; implemented pagination and estimation of cost-per-request for the max_estimated_cost_per_k filter.
- openai-compat layer: updated selection logic to prefer exact capability matches, then compatibility-flagged fallbacks, then nearest-latency alternative. This avoids regression for streaming clients while giving a predictable fallback path.
- SDK updates: python and ts SDKs gain ModelCatalog.list_models() optional filters.
- docs: new reference doc with examples and migration guidance.

Testing: Added unit tests for filter logic, integration tests exercising the openai-compat routing, and a small perf smoke to ensure the filter path does not add >1ms median overhead. CI initially caught a flaky kv-cache test unrelated to the change; rerun resolved it.

Rollout plan: Feature is behind a feature flag (model_catalog_capabilities_v2) and enabled for internal and canary tenants first. Console uses the new fields only when the flag is on. After 2 weeks of canary telemetry (stability and no client errors), we will flip default and update external SDK releases.

Related: ENG-4821 (catalog UX for capability discovery), SUP-188 (customer reported mismatch between advertised function calling and actual routing), docs PR will follow to expand examples.

Notes for reviewers: focus on the filter combinator correctness (api/handlers/models/filter.go), openai-compat selection priority (pkg/openai/selector.go) and the SDK bindings. The JSON snippet above is illustrative; exact field names are in the updated API doc file.

title: Introduce probabilistic span sampler and kernel branch-counter hooks for adaptive scheduler insights
preview:
Context and motivation: We see intermittent scheduler degradation for long-tail requests where KV cache hits are correlated with kernel branch behavior and micro-stalls. Existing NVTX and epoch counters capture timing but not branch-level dynamics. This PR introduces a probabilistic (weighted) span sampler that biases capture toward requests with high KV lookup latency and late-batch positions, plus a small set of kernel branch/mispredict counters exposed through the telemetry pipeline so dashboard panels can correlate scheduler decisions with low-level CPU/GPU branch behavior.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a lightweight probabilistic span sampler (runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.{cc,h}) that samples at enqueue time based on configurable weights: kv_hit_latency (ms), batch_index, and recent_retry_count. The sampler emits full NVTX-range + span payload for sampled requests only, keeping steady-state overhead near 0.3% at 95th percentile load in our microbench. Introduces kernel branch counters (telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.cpp) that aggregate branch-mispredict and branch-instruction counts at the scheduler worker boundary; counters are aggregated per-epoch and batched to the existing telemetry pipeline. Updates perf-dashboard json (observability/dashboards/weighted-span-sampler.json) with panels that join span traces and branch counters, plus a dedicated query to highlight high-latency KV lookups with elevated branch-mispredict ratios. Includes unit tests and an integration microbench (bench/micro/sampler_adaptive_sched_bench.cc) that reproduces regression patterns.

Checklist (author): - [x] Implementation of weighted span sampler core - [x] Kernel branch counters and telemetry export - [x] Dashboard panels and query snippets - [x] Deterministic microbench and unit tests - [x] Docs for ops on how to interpret panels and sampler tuning

Changed areas (high-level file list): runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.cc, runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.h, telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.cpp, telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.h, observability/dashboards/weighted-span-sampler.json, bench/micro/sampler_adaptive_sched_bench.cc, tests/sampling/test_weighted_sampler.cpp, docs/observability/sampler.md, BUILD.bazel (telemetry exports).

Test/CI notes: CI performed three runs. First run failed a flaky perf-test (bench micro reliability) due to environmental jitter; second run re-ran the flaky job and passed. All unit tests and integration tests pass locally and in CI. End-to-end telemetry e2e smoke was validated in staging with a synthetic traffic pattern; dashboard queries returned expected joins.

Review conversation (selected highlights):
- Diego Martins (review): "Nice approach — weighting by KV hit latency makes sense. Two asks: (1) make the sampler weight function pluggable (so ops can try other heuristics), and (2) please add an opt-out runtime flag for customers concerned about any additional counters."
- Ava Chen (author reply): "Pluggable weight function is implemented via a SamplerStrategy interface (see sampling/strategy.h); default strategy remains the weighted heuristic. Added runtime flag --telemetry.disable_branch_counters with documentation and feature-gate check."
- Priya Patel (review): "Telemetry export looks good; can you add a dashboard note explaining expected baseline ranges for branch-mispredict ratio? Otherwise panels are noisy for new tenants."
- Ava Chen: "Added doc section with baseline guidance and a small script to compute baseline from a 1-hour sample."
- Marcus Lee (approval): "+1 after tests re-run — please squash on merge to keep history tidy."

Performance measurements: Microbench in bench/micro shows sampler overhead <0.5% p95 request CPU and memory delta in a 64-core staging cluster; sampling rate default is 0.1% (configurable). Branch counters add ~0.2% overhead at aggregation boundary when enabled. Combined telemetry ingestion size increased by ~0.8% at default sampling rate. Long-tail test: when enabling weighted sampling, traces for 99.9th percentile latency requests increased by ~18x (samples) enabling better RCA visibility.

Operational notes and config: Default config retains existing telemetry costs: sampler default rate 0.001 (0.1%), weight_exponent 1.2, kv_latency_threshold_ms 1.0. Operators can change strategy via telemetry.sampler.strategy=<name> and disable branch counters via telemetry.disable_branch_counters=true. Dashboard docs include PromQL/ESQL examples to compute mispredict_ratio = branch_mispredicts / branch_instructions.

Potential risks and mitigation: Branch counters require a small kernel shim for CPUs on some older distros — guarded behind feature gate and disabled by default. We instrument aggregation carefully to avoid contention (batch buffer + single flush per-epoch). If customers enable aggressive sampling, telemetry costs can increase; guidance added to docs.

Merge notes: Squash merge performed by Marcus Lee after CI re-run and approvals. Changelog entry added under Observability / Telemetry.

Related work and follow-ups: Follow-up tickets created to (1) explore GPU-side branch sampling equivalents (ENG-3532) and (2) a background replayer that uses sampled spans to auto-propose scheduler policy tweaks (ENG-3551).

title: Hybrid quant execution paths: FP8-aware kernels, INT8 dequant+matmul fusion and scheduler refactor
preview: Motivation: customers running large conversational workloads push two axes of optimization simultaneously: cheaper quantized kernels (INT8/FP8) and smarter scheduling to preserve latency SLOs when batching. This PR implements a hybrid execution strategy that (1) adds FP8-aware matmul kernels with per-channel scale metadata, (2) implements an INT8 dequant+matmul fusion execution path to avoid intermediate FP buffers when upstream weights are INT8 and downstream ops permit FP accumulation, and (3) refactors the request scheduler to expose an adaptive batch window and preemptive slotting to reduce head-of-line and tail latency during bursty traffic. Summary of changes: new kernel files under serving/runtime/kernels/{fp8_matmul.cc,fp8_matmul.h,quant_fusion.cc}, changes to kernel registry to prefer fused path when data layout and accumulation type are compatible, scheduler refactor in serving/runtime/scheduler/{adaptive_window.rs,slotting.rs} with a conservative default policy that preserves existing latency behavior, KV cache interaction updates in serving/runtime/cache to bypass copy-on-dequant for fused paths, continuous batching adjustments to prefer short windows for chat-like traffic and longer windows for bulk embed workloads, micro-bench harness and bench scripts added to infra/bench/quant_hybrid. Commits (high-level): 1) initial FP8 kernel implementation and microbench harness, 2) INT8 dequant+matmul fusion path and kernel registry integration, 3) scheduler refactor and policy configuration knobs, 4) KV cache bypass and tests, 5) docs + benchmarks + small followups. Files changed (high level): serving/runtime/kernels/* (new kernels and tests), serving/runtime/registry.cc (selection logic), serving/runtime/scheduler/* (refactor and policy), serving/runtime/cache/* (KV cache bypass), infra/bench/* (bench scripts), docs/perf/quant-hybrid.md (guidance and numbers), tests/unit/* (numerics and scheduler unit tests). Benchmarks: end-to-end microbench on a T4/80GB-equivalent collector shows FP8 matmul 1.8-2.1x faster than FP16 baseline for long sequences and 2.3-2.6x for short dense matmuls; INT8 fused path reduced memory pressure and improved throughput by ~1.4x in mixed workloads. Tail latency (p95-p99) improved by 10-20% across our chat-like workload mix when the adaptive batch window is enabled with default conservative params. Cost projection: for a typical chat endpoint these changes reduce per-token GPU cycles by ~18% at our sampled workload, with larger wins for model families that compress well to FP8. Testing and CI: added unit tests for numeric parity (relative error tracking with random seeds), integration tests for fused path selection, and scheduler unit tests with simulated arrival patterns. CI initially showed flakiness on scheduler timing tests due to coarse clocks in the test harness; tests stabilized after tightening timing expectations and adding deterministic seeds. Review conversation highlights: Liam O'Connor: "Numerical stability seems OK on the sampled seeds but please add a stress test with longer sequences and underflow/overflow envelopes." Author (Ava Chen): "Added stress test harness and long-sequence seeds, documented the worst-case relative error and safe fallbacks to FP16 accumulation in the registry when scale overflow is detected." Grace Patel: "Scheduler changes look good; can we make the adaptive window configurable per-route?" Author: "Yes, added per-route knobs with sane defaults and a global override for Dedicated customers." Rohit Mehra: "Bench numbers look promising; please add scripts to reproduce and commit summaries of raw results." Author: "Bench scripts committed to infra/bench with a README pointing to s3 artifact locations; raw CSVs attached to Linear ticket ENG-5821." CI & approvals: initial CI run failed on flaky timing test, second run passed; reviewers Grace and Rohit approved after perf/regression signoff; Liam gave an approve-after-changes tag once stress tests added. Migration and config: new config flags added to runtime config schema: quant.hybrid.enabled, quant.hybrid.strict_scale_check, scheduler.adaptive_window.enabled, scheduler.adaptive_window.max_ms. Default values preserve previous behavior unless quant.hybrid.enabled is set. Backwards compatibility: the fused path is opt-in via registry selection and falls back to legacy dequant path on numeric anomalies; no customer-facing API changes. Documentation: docs/perf/quant-hybrid.md contains recommendations on when to enable FP8/INT8 paths, how to tune scheduler knobs for chat vs embed workloads, and an appendix with reproduced bench numbers and command lines. Follow-ups: 1) larger FP8 coverage for other kernels (conv1d, rnn) tracked in ENG-5830, 2) investigate hardware-specific low-level kernels for A100/A800 in a follow-up PR, 3) expand regression testing to include stochastic load patterns in canary. Merge: squashed and merged to main on 2024-11-21 after approvals and green CI. Artifacts: bench outputs and CSVs uploaded to s3://redwood-ci/bench/pr-31207/.

title: Fork-and-Join rollout controller with stable session affinity
preview:
Summary: Introduce a new Fork-and-Join rollout controller and CRD (ForkJoinRollout) that allows operators to split traffic into multiple parallel rollout paths (forks), run independent validation windows, then atomically join a winning path back into the primary serving graph while preserving stable session affinity and KV cache locality. Motivation: Some experiments require running two or more independent model variants for overlapping cohorts and then merging the best-performing variant into production without user-visible churn or cache thrash. Existing canary and shadow primitives support linear progressive rollouts but do not provide first-class semantics for forked experiments and deterministic joins. This PR implements the control plane and runtime hooks to enable fork-and-join workflows with safety fences and rollback tracebacks. Changes: Commits: 1) Add CRD and API surface for ForkJoinRollout with fields: forks[], joinPolicy{windowMs, quorum, metricsGates}, affinity{cookieHeader, hashSalt}, rollbackTraceTTL 2) Implement controller reconciliation loop: deterministic cohort hashing, fork assignment, per-fork observation windows, metric aggregation, join decision, and transactional switch-over. 3) Runtime integration: routing plugin that honors session affinity cookie/header to route existing sessions consistently to their assigned fork across restarts. 4) KV cache migration helpers that pin or warm keys for transitioning cohorts to avoid cache cold-start spikes at join. 5) Metrics and alerting: new rollout.* metrics, join_decision_latency, affinity_mismatch_count, cache_warmup_effect. 6) E2E tests: simulated traffic harness, chaos test (controller restarts), and load test to validate no-traffic-glitch join. Files changed (high-level):
- k8s/crds/forkjoinrollout.yaml: new CRD and schema.
- controllers/forkjoin/controller.go: main reconciliation implementation and state machine.
- controllers/forkjoin/affinity.go: deterministic hashing and cookie utility.
- runtime/router/plugin_forkjoin.go: router plugin that preserves affinity and implements transactional join.
- runtime/cache/warmup.go: helpers to migrate/warm keys during join.
- observability/metrics_rollouts.prom: new metrics.
- tests/e2e/forkjoin/harness.go: traffic harness and validators.
- docs/deployments/forkjoin.md: operator guide and examples.
- scripts/bench/forkjoin_load_test.sh: benchmark harness configs.
- go.mod/go.sum and small refactors in existing rollout packages.
Review thread summary:
- Ravi: "Controller looks solid but we need stronger invariants around rollbackTraceTTL to avoid orphaned sessions." Author (Maya): "Added TTL enforcement in reconciliation and a periodic sweep task; documented behaviour."
- Sofia: "Affinity cookie name collisions with other RBAC cookies are possible in some proxies." Author: "Namespace-prefixed cookie names and a proxy integration guideline added to docs."
- Ethan: "Will join be atomic from a client perspective? What about in-flight KV cache updates?" Author: "Join is transactional at routing layer: existing sessions continue hitting assigned variant until their TTL expires; new sessions post-join get routed to joined variant. KV warmup reduces cold-start; we added a brief overlap window where both variants are served to smooth cache state."
Requested changes and iterations:
- Added unit tests for hashing function and cookie header fallbacks.
- Added additional metrics for debugging affinity mismatches.
- Tightened concurrency handling in controller reconciliation to avoid double-assigning cohorts under controller duplication.
CI: Initial CI run flagged a flaky e2e test on the harness timeout.
Fixes: increased harness timeout and added retries for the chaos step.
Final CI status: all checks passed on rerun (unit, integration, e2e, lint, go vet).

Motivation: Some experiments require running two or more independent model variants for overlapping cohorts and then merging the best-performing variant into production without user-visible churn or cache thrash. Existing canary and shadow primitives support linear progressive rollouts but do not provide first-class semantics for forked experiments and deterministic joins. This PR implements the control plane and runtime hooks to enable fork-and-join workflows with safety fences and rollback tracebacks. title: streaming-reconstruction-guard-and-toolcall-stable-handback
preview: Motivation: We observed a class of in-production incidents where SSE fragment splits inside escaped JSON strings or multi-byte UTF-8 sequences could cause downstream consumers to see re-ordered function/tool call boundaries or emit malformed partial JSON. This PR introduces a lightweight runtime 'reconstruction guard' that assigns per-fragment fidelity credits and a deterministic handback protocol to ensure toolcall boundaries remain ordered across retransmit/replay and partial-frame repair. Summary of changes: 1) runtime/streaming: add FragmentFidelityLedger and a reconstruction guard that tracks credit windows and stalls handback until a safe boundary is observed or the ledger times out; 2) sse/parser: introduce escape-aware tail rewriter to avoid splitting inside escapes and normalize trailing delimiters before emission; 3) compat/openai: emit X-Redwood-Handback-Fidelity header and annotate stream events with handback_id so OpenAI-compat clients can prefer resumable handoff points; 4) protocol: deterministic toolcall handoff algorithm that converts ephemeral function-call tokens into ordered handback checkpoints to prevent interleaving when fragments arrive out-of-order; 5) tests: large suite of synthetic fragment replays and property tests that simulate jittered, coalesced, and reordered chunks. Implementation notes: The reconstruction guard uses a small in-memory ledger per connection (bounded to 32 entries) and a sliding credit window to avoid unbounded buffering. When ledger credit is insufficient, the connection flushes an escape-normalized delimiter and advances a handback checkpoint; this behavior preserves low-latency in common cases while preventing rare misorders. The OpenAI-compat annotations are backward compatible: older clients ignore handback_id and continue to operate normally. Checklist: - [x] Core runtime changes implemented and covered by unit tests - [x] End-to-end tests with python and ts SDKs - [x] Backwards compatibility validated against existing OpenAI-compat integration tests - [x] Performance microbenchmarks show <0.6% median latency regression on p4d for synthetic workloads - [x] Feature flagged behind runtime.feature.streaming_reconstruct_guard rollout plan: feature off in production initial cut; enable for 10% traffic with canary instrumentation and latency/error dashboards; expand to 100% after 48h with no regressions. Related: ENG-2407 describes incident notes and replay corpus. This description includes small illustrative pseudo-patch: "if (fragment.endsWith('\\') || insideEscape(fragment)) normalizeEscapeTail(fragment)" which is implemented in sse/parser/tail_rewrite.go.

title: delta-driven pin throttling and tiered reuse allocator for paged KV
preview: Motivation: Our paged KV block manager saw high tail latency and frequent pin-induced stalls when conversational workloads produce short bursts of focused hot prefixes. Existing heuristics pinned aggressively on first-detect hotness and coalesced eagerly, which increased contention and caused kernel scheduling jitter. This change introduces a delta-driven pin heuristic plus a lightweight tiered reuse allocator to smooth pin activity and defer expensive coalesces into the background.
text: Motivation: Our paged KV block manager saw high tail latency and frequent pin-induced stalls when conversational workloads produce short bursts of focused hot prefixes. Existing heuristics pinned aggressively on first-detect hotness and coalesced eagerly, which increased contention and caused kernel scheduling jitter. This change introduces a delta-driven pin heuristic plus a lightweight tiered reuse allocator to smooth pin activity and defer expensive coalesces into the background.

title: Safe Rejoin Coordinator with Connection Epoch Fence
preview:
Motivation: During a cross-region incident we observed a small class of degradations where connections that rejoined quickly after transient network flaps caused KV cache overwrite and high tail latency spikes. This PR introduces a Safe Rejoin Coordinator and a Connection Epoch Fence to ensure rejoining nodes validate epoch affinity before accepting requests that may mutate or repopulate per-connection KV cache entries. The change reduces risk of stale-kv stomping and provides a short quiesce window for region rejoin sequences.

Scope: single runtime-side coordinator service + lightweight epoch fence protocol and control-plane signals for coordinated rejoin. The implementation intentionally keeps the runtime fast-path unchanged for steady state; the coordinator only participates during node state transitions.

What this PR changes: 1) runtime/rejoin/coordinator: new coordinator that tracks node epochs and issues timeboxed rejoin permits; 2) runtime/kv/fence: epoch-checks on KV-preserve paths to reject/forward ambiguous rejoin traffic; 3) controlplane/handlers: small handshake to annotate node reprovision events with epoch tokens; 4) ops tooling: a console toggle to enable conservative quiesce windows during rollouts.

Testing and validation: extensive unit tests for epoch validation logic, integration test that simulates multi-region reconnects with packet loss and ensures no KV overwrite, and a perf soak showing <1% p50 impact in steady-state. CI includes a canary job that runs the rejoin scenario harness.

Rollout plan: feature-gate off by default on main; staged rollout to production via the console toggle. Initial rollout will target non-critical regions (eu-west-2, ap-south-1) for 48 hours, then gradual expansion with telemetry checks for KV-inconsistency metrics. Runbook included for emergency rollback.

Checklist: [x] Unit tests added; [x] Integration scenario added to perf lab; [x] Console toggle implemented; [x] Release notes drafted.

Notes: This is designed as a low-risk path to reduce a very specific class of tail latency incidents tied to rejoin races. It is not intended to replace longer-term topology upgrades but mitigates immediate customer impact and enables safer hot restarts across regions.
